name: Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi
epithet: The Man Who Carried His Past
title: Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi (RA): The Man Who Carried His Past
meta_description: The story of Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi (RA), who stood in the crowd at a Companion's killing, then came to Islam and lived as one of its quietest, most just men. A life shaped by repentance.
keywords: Saeed ibn Amir, story of Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi, governor of Homs, Umar ibn al-Khattab, repentance in Islam, sahaba stories
lede: Imagine sitting beside an old man in the mosque of Madinah. He is quiet. He has only one story he tells about himself, and it is not an easy one to hear.

This is the story of Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi (may Allah be pleased with him): a man who once stood in a hostile crowd, who came to Islam carrying that memory, and who spent the rest of his life trying to deserve forgiveness.

paras: Saeed was a young man of Quraysh, from the noble and wealthy clan of Banu Jumah. His was not a family with mild feelings about Islam. His uncle had been a fierce enemy of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, killed at Badr, a man so bitter that the Qur'an describes the regret of his kind on the Day of Judgment. His tribe was at the center of the fight against the new faith. Saeed himself had not fought in the early battles. He was a younger man, simply there, swept along with his people.

Then came the day a captured Companion, Khubayb ibn Adi (may Allah be pleased with him), was brought out to be killed at a place called Tanim. He had been one of the teachers the Prophet ﷺ sent out, and now a mob gathered to watch him die. Saeed went out with them, and he stood close enough to see everything. He would never forget it.

Khubayb asked to pray two short units, then made them short on purpose. "If I did not fear you would think I was afraid," he said, "I would have prayed longer." As they raised him up and the blows fell, they taunted him: would he not rather Muhammad ﷺ were in his place? Khubayb answered that he would not wish the Prophet ﷺ to be pricked by a single thorn to save himself and his family. Then, dying, he prayed against the people who were killing him.

That prayer struck the crowd with such force that grown men threw themselves to the ground. Saeed went home that night and could not rest. He replayed it over and over: the calm, the conviction, the strength that nothing they did could shake. He had been a bystander, not a killer. But he had been there, among his people, while it happened, and he had not lifted a finger to stop it. That weight would never fully leave him.

Something in him broke loose. One day Saeed called out to the assembly of Quraysh and told them plainly that he wanted nothing more to do with any of it: not their idols, not their ways, not what they had done. He declared himself free of all of it, and he set out for Madinah.

The timing tells you everything about him. People came to Islam in waves, and the easy waves came late, after victory was certain. The largest groups embraced Islam only once Makkah had fallen and there was no longer any reason to resist. Saeed came before all of that, in the years when the Muslims were most vulnerable and there was nothing to gain by joining them, and much to lose. He chose that moment, and the choosing was a sign of how sincere he was.
The Qur'an itself marks this difference between those who came early and those who came once it was safe:

ayah.text: Why should you not give for God's cause when God alone will inherit what is in the heavens and earth? Those who gave and fought before the triumph are not like others: they are greater in rank than those who gave and fought afterwards. But God has promised a good reward to all of them: God is fully aware of all that you do.

paras: Saeed came from money. His clan were among the elites of Makkah, used to comfort and standing. When he reached the Prophet ﷺ, he set all of that down. He did not want the wealth of this world, and he could not be softened or bought with it. He wanted only to be among the poor of the Companions.

He left behind only one narration from the Prophet ﷺ, and it is telling. He narrated that the poor believers will be brought forward on the Day of Judgment, lined up in their groups, and asked to account for themselves. They will say they were left nothing to be questioned about. With nothing to answer for, they will enter Paradise ahead of others. Saeed wanted to be one of them, so he gave away what came to him and kept almost nothing.

This is what drew Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) to him. Umar was not easy to impress, and he distrusted men who loved comfort or chased position. In Saeed he found the opposite: a man of real piety who wanted no part of any of it. The two grew close, and they made a habit of advising one another for the sake of Allah.

When Umar became the leader of the Muslims, Saeed gave him counsel that is still remembered. "Fear Allah regarding the people," he said, "and do not fear the people regarding Allah. Do not let your words depart from your deeds, for the best words are those followed by action. Take the truth wherever it leads you, and do not fear, in the way of Allah, the blame of anyone who blames."

Then Umar repaid the favor in his own way. He appointed Saeed governor of Homs, a great city in Syria. Saeed begged him not to: "Leave me to the mosque, leave me to my worship." Umar refused. "You people put this burden on my neck and then left me. By Allah, I will not let you go."

Umar offered him a salary so he would never need to take from anyone, a practice he kept so his governors could not be bribed. Saeed declined even that. What little came to him from the public treasury, he said, was already more than he needed. As he sent him off, Umar told him plainly: "I am not sending you to beat the people or dishonor them. I am sending you to live among them justly, and to share fairly what Allah provides."

Homs was so full of complaints that people half-jokingly called it a second Kufa. So when Umar visited and asked the people about their governor, he braced himself, then made a quiet prayer: that his good opinion of Saeed would not be proven wrong. They said Saeed was good, but they had four things to raise. Umar called him forward to answer each one in person.

First: he came out to them late in the morning. Saeed admitted, reluctantly, that he had no servant. Each morning he kneaded his own dough and baked his own bread for his family before he could come out. Second: he did not answer them at night. He had given his nights to his Lord, he said, and kept his days for the people. Third: he took one day a month away from them. He owned a single garment, and on that day he washed it and waited for it to dry before he could be seen.
Each answer left the people quieter than the last. They had come with complaints, and every complaint turned out to be a window into how little their governor kept for himself.
The fourth was different. Sometimes, they said, Saeed fainted in the middle of sitting with them. He would simply lose consciousness. They were less angry now than worried: what was wrong with him?

Saeed told them what almost none of them knew. He had been there at Tanim, among the crowd, when Khubayb was killed. He had watched it happen and done nothing. "Every time I remember that scene," he said, "and that I did not help him, I am afraid Allah has not forgiven me, and I pass out."

Umar understood. He was perhaps the only man in that gathering who did, because he remembered those years and what they had cost. He praised Allah, who had not let his good opinion of Saeed be wrong. The man who had stood in the crowd had grown into one of the most just leaders the Muslims had, and he still carried that one moment everywhere he went.

More than once, Umar sent Saeed money, a thousand dinars at a time, to ease his hardship. The first time, Saeed told his wife that something had entered their home that would ruin it, and asked her to help him give it all away to the poor. She agreed.

Years later, when another gift came, his wife thought at last they could settle their debts. Saeed turned to her with a question: what if they did something better with it? What if they gave it to those who came to them in need, even when their own need was greater, and made it a beautiful loan to Allah? She said yes.

On another occasion, when someone insulted him, he did not strike back. He only warned the man gently that he had nearly earned the curse of the angels, and could have spared himself by holding his tongue. That was the harshest exchange anyone could recall from his years of leadership.

He and his wife became known for filling small pouches and passing them quietly to the poor. Saeed ibn Amir died in the year 19 after the Hijra and was buried in Syria. He had spent decades doing good, and to the end he was still unsure whether one old wound had been forgiven.

lessons_intro: His story is not about a single brave act. It is about what a person does with a heavy past, over a long and quiet life.

lessons.bold: Repentance can become a way of living.
lessons.text: Saeed did not just feel sorry once. The memory drove him for the rest of his life: to give more, to judge justly, to keep almost nothing for himself.

lessons.bold: A clear conscience is worth more than comfort.
lessons.text: He came from wealth and walked away from it, turning down salary and gifts so that nothing could buy him and nothing would have to be accounted for.

lessons.bold: Good leadership is service, not power.
lessons.text: His complaints were that he baked his own bread and washed his only garment. He led a city while keeping the life of its poorest people.

lessons.bold: Hope and fear can live together.
lessons.text: He spent decades in good works and still feared he was not forgiven. That fear did not crush him; it kept him moving toward Allah.

faq.q: Who was Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi?
faq.a: A Companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the clan of Banu Jumah in Makkah. He came to Islam before the conquest of Makkah and later served as the governor of Homs in Syria, known for his justice and his deliberate poverty.

faq.q: Why did Saeed faint in his gatherings?
faq.a: As a young man, before Islam, he had stood in the crowd that watched the Companion Khubayb ibn Adi (RA) be killed, and he did nothing. Whenever he remembered that scene, he feared Allah had not forgiven him, and he would lose consciousness.

faq.q: Why did Umar value Saeed so highly?
faq.a: Umar (RA) distrusted men who loved wealth or chased position. Saeed wanted neither. His sincerity, integrity, and refusal of comfort impressed Umar deeply, and the two advised one another for the sake of Allah.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Saeed ibn Amir?
faq.a: That repentance can shape an entire life, that a clear conscience outweighs comfort, that real leadership is service, and that hope and fear of Allah can carry a person forward together.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us carry something we wish we had not done, or had done differently. Saeed's life shows what that weight can become. He did not let it harden into despair, and he did not pretend it away. He let it turn him into a more honest, more giving, more careful man, year after quiet year.

His example asks a gentle question of our own day: what are we doing with our regret? It can sit on us like a stone, or it can push us, the way it pushed him, toward worship, toward generosity, toward treating people justly. A heavy past does not have to define the rest of a life. In his case, it quietly made him one of the best of men.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 57:10). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
There was an old man in the mosque of Madinah.

He was quiet. He told only one story about himself.

And it was not an easy one to hear.

As a young man, he stood in the crowd that killed a Companion of the Prophet ﷺ.

He did not lift a finger to stop it. He never forgot it.

He watched Khubayb (RA) face death without flinching.

"I would not wish the Prophet ﷺ to be pricked by a single thorn to save myself."

That conviction broke something open in Saeed.

He came to Islam before the conquest of Makkah.

Not in the easy years. In the years when there was nothing to gain by it, and everything to lose.

He came from wealth. He gave it all up.

When Umar (RA) made him governor and offered him a salary, he refused. He wanted nothing the world could use to buy him.

His people had four complaints about their governor.

He baked his own bread. He owned one garment. He gave his nights to his Lord.

Each complaint was a window into how little he kept.

The fourth: sometimes he fainted while sitting with them.

"I was there when Khubayb was killed, and I did not help him. I am afraid Allah has not forgiven me."

**Repentance can become a way of living.**

Decades of good, still carrying one old wound. It did not crush him. It kept him moving toward Allah.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi (may Allah be pleased with him) had one story he told about himself, and it was not a proud one. As a young man, before Islam, he stood in the crowd that watched a Companion of the Prophet ﷺ be killed, and he did nothing.

That memory followed him for life. He came to Islam in its hardest years, gave away his wealth, refused a governor's salary, and led a city while baking his own bread. And still, decades later, he would faint when he remembered that one day, afraid he had not been forgiven.

His regret did not crush him. It made him one of the most just and giving men of his time.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The Companion who fainted from an old memory
email.alt_subject: He carried one day for the rest of his life
email.preview: Saeed ibn Amir stood in the wrong crowd once. Then he spent decades trying to deserve forgiveness.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There was an old man who used to sit quietly in the mosque of Madinah. He told only one story about himself, and it was not a proud one.

His name was Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi (may Allah be pleased with him). As a young man, before Islam, he had stood in the crowd that watched a Companion of the Prophet ﷺ, Khubayb ibn Adi, be killed. He did not take part. But he was there, among his people, and he did nothing to stop it.

He watched Khubayb face death without fear, certain of where he was going. He heard him say he would not wish the Prophet ﷺ to be pricked by a single thorn to save himself. Saeed went home that night and could not rest.

Something broke loose in him. He walked away from his people and came to Islam, and he came early, in the years when the Muslims were most vulnerable and there was nothing to gain by joining them. He gave away his wealth. When Umar (may Allah be pleased with him) later made him governor of a great city and offered him a salary, he refused it. He wanted nothing the world could use to buy him.

His people once brought four complaints about him to Umar. He came out late, because he had no servant and baked his own bread. He did not answer them at night, because his nights belonged to his Lord. He took one day a month away, because he owned a single garment and had to wash it. Each complaint turned out to be a window into how little he kept for himself.

The fourth was different. Sometimes he fainted while sitting with them. He explained: "I was there when Khubayb was killed, and I did not help him. Every time I remember it, I am afraid Allah has not forgiven me."

He had decades of good behind him by then. And still he carried that one day everywhere he went. His regret did not crush him. It made him more honest, more giving, more just. It kept him moving toward Allah.

Most of us carry something we wish we had done differently. His life is a quiet question about what we do with it.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: There was an old man who used to sit quietly in the mosque of Madinah. If you did not know him, you might guess he had a difficult past. He told only one story about himself, and it was not an easy one to hear.

His name was Saeed ibn Amir al-Jumahi (may Allah be pleased with him). As a young man, before Islam, he stood in the crowd at a place called Tanim, where a captured Companion named Khubayb was to be killed. Saeed did not take part. But he was there, among his people, and he watched.

He saw Khubayb pray two calm units and then keep them short on purpose, so no one would think he was afraid. He heard them taunt him, asking if he would rather the Prophet ﷺ were in his place, and he heard Khubayb answer that he would not wish the Prophet ﷺ to be pricked by a single thorn to save himself. Then Khubayb, dying, prayed against the people who were killing him, and the prayer struck the crowd so hard that men fell to the ground.

Saeed went home and could not rest. Something broke loose in him. One day he stood before his people and told them he wanted nothing more to do with any of it, and he set out for Madinah. He came to Islam early, in the years when there was nothing to gain by it, and that was the measure of how sincere he was.

He came from a wealthy family, and he set all of it down. He wanted to be among the poor of the Companions. When Umar made him governor of a great city, he begged to be left to his worship instead, and when Umar offered him a salary, he refused it. He baked his own bread, owned a single garment, and gave away every gift that came to him.

His people once complained that he sometimes fainted while sitting with them. Saeed explained why. He had been there when Khubayb was killed, and he had not helped him. "Every time I remember that scene," he said, "I am afraid Allah has not forgiven me." By then he had decades of good behind him, and still he carried that one day everywhere he went.

He and his wife grew famous for filling small pouches of money and passing them quietly to the poor, even when their own need was greater, treating it as a loan to Allah. Saeed died in the year 19 after the Hijra. To the end, he was still hoping one old wound had been forgiven.
storybook.pullquote: Every time I remember that scene, I am afraid Allah has not forgiven me.
storybook.reflection: Most of us carry something we wish we had not done. Saeed shows what that weight can become. He did not let his regret harden into despair or pretend it away. He let it turn him, year after quiet year, into a more honest and more giving man. A heavy past does not have to define the rest of a life.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
